rule_text: A mean-reverting strategy on ES that enters on Bollinger Band extremes (±2 standard deviations) and exits when price reverts toward the moving average (±1 standard deviation).
entry_conditions: Price exceeds +2 moving standard deviations above the moving average (short entry)
Price falls below -2 moving standard deviations below the moving average (long entry)

parameters.threshold: ±2 standard deviations for entry, ±1 standard deviation for exit
parameters.backtest_sharpe: 3.0 without transaction costs; -3.0 with 1 basis point transaction costs

exit_conditions: Exit the position when price reverts back to within 1 moving standard deviation of the moving average

rule_text: Buy the 10 lowest-priced stocks from the 1,000 largest stocks by market cap at year-start and hold them equally-weighted for one year.

Identify the 10 stocks with the lowest closing prices at the beginning of the year
Equal initial capital allocation across all 10 selected stocks
Enter positions at the start of the year
exit_conditions: Exit all positions after holding for one year (1/2/2002 in example)

exit_conditions: Close both IGE long and SPY short positions simultaneously in November 2007

rule_text: A pairs trading strategy that identifies a hedge ratio between two correlated assets (GLD and GDX) using linear regression on historical data, then constructs a mean-reverting spread.
entry_conditions: Calculate hedge ratio using ordinary least squares (OLS) regression of adjusted close prices over a 252-day training period
Construct spread as: Spread = GLD price - (hedge ratio × GDX price)
Monitor spread for mean reversion signals on the test set (after day 252)
exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: A mean-reversion pairs trading strategy that goes long the spread when it drops below -2 standard deviations and goes short when it rises above +2 standard deviations, exiting when it mean-reverts within ±1 standard deviation.
entry_conditions: Z-score of spread is less than or equal to -2 standard deviations (long entry)
Z-score of spread is greater than or equal to +2 standard deviations (short entry)

parameters.threshold: 2 standard deviations for entry; 1 standard deviation for exit

exit_conditions: Absolute z-score of spread is less than or equal to 1 standard deviation (mean reversion threshold)

exit_conditions: Exit both positions (set to zero) when exit signal is generated

rule_text: Equal-weighted mean reversion strategy that shorts stocks outperforming the market index and longs stocks underperforming it, with position sizes proportional to the negative distance from the market return.
entry_conditions: Calculate daily returns for each stock
Calculate equal-weighted market index daily return across all stocks
For each stock, compute the deviation of its daily return from the market daily return

Assign position weight proportional to the negative of this deviation (stocks lagging the market receive positive weights for long positions; stocks outperforming receive negative weights for short positions)
Normalize weights by the count of stocks with valid price data
exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

usefulfortheoptimalallocationofcapitalandforthedetermination
of the optimal leverage, but also for risk management. In
rule_text: Reduce position size whenever the portfolio experiences a trading loss, and increase position size when the strategy is profitable, based on Kelly formula optimal leverage principles.

exit_conditions: Portfolio experiences a trading loss, triggering position size reduction
Kelly formula recalculation indicates reduced optimal leverage due to losses

Reduce portfolio leverage to account for continuous rebalancing requirements and transaction costs

rule_text: Size leverage using the smaller of half-Kelly leverage or maximum leverage derived from historical worst-case drawdown analysis
entry_conditions: Position sizing is determined by comparing two leverage limits
Calculate half-Kelly leverage based on strategy win rate and risk/reward
Calculate maximum tolerable leverage by dividing maximum acceptable one-period drawdown on equity by the maximum historical one-period loss
exit_conditions: Rebalance portfolio at end of every period (one week, one day, or one hour) according to Kelly formula
Exit if drawdown exceeds maximum tolerable one-period drawdown on equity
target_regimes: Extreme events and tail risk management

rule_text: Use stop losses when in a momentum/trending regime (driven by news or fundamental deterioration), but avoid them in mean-reverting regimes where prices recover naturally.
entry_conditions: Price movement is caused by news or fundamental reasons (e.g., company revenue deterioration)
Market is identified to be in a momentum regime rather than mean-reverting regime
exit_conditions: Apply stop loss when in momentum regime to avoid larger losses (prices expected to worsen)
Avoid stop loss or use wider stops in mean-reverting regime to allow position recovery

Do NOT abruptly shut down the model on large drawdowns; instead reduce leverage systematically

rule_text: Dynamically adjust leverage and capital allocation based on model performance using the Kelly formula, reducing exposure during losing periods and avoiding rapid increases during winning periods to prevent overleveraging.
entry_conditions: Model is generating trading signals (entry conditions vary by underlying strategy)

parameters.threshold: Kelly formula dictates maximum leverage; maintain leverage below Kelly recommendation

exit_conditions: Reduce capital allocation gradually when model enters losing period
Avoid rapid leverage increases during profitable runs to prevent overleveraging
Account for fat-tail event risk by maintaining conservative leverage margins below Kelly formula limits

rule_text: Use a perceptron neural network to combine multiple holding-period rules (including ±2% rules) with optimized weights trained on a moving window of historical data to generate buy-and-sell signals.
entry_conditions: Perceptron trained on moving window of historical data outputs a buy signal

rule_text: A perceptron machine learning model combines multiple trading rules with optimized linear weights to generate daily buy, sell, or do-nothing signals based on a moving window of recent data.
entry_conditions: Perceptron model outputs a buy signal based on linearly weighted combination of multiple component trading rules

exit_conditions: Perceptron model outputs a sell signal at end of period

rule_text: Form a mean-reverting pairs trade by longing one security and shorting a cointegrated partner in the same industry group at a ratio determined by regression fit to create a stationary spread.
entry_conditions: Identify two securities in the same industry group that are cointegrated
Perform regression fit of the two time series to determine the hedge ratio

Short the hedge ratio number of shares of the second security (e.g., 1.6766 shares of GDX)
The resulting spread between the two positions forms a stationary time series

rule_text: Trade the spread between two cointegrated securities in the same industry group as a mean-reverting strategy when the spread deviates from its mean.
entry_conditions: Two price series are confirmed to be cointegrated using the cointegrating augmented Dickey-Fuller (cadf) test
Optimal hedge ratio is calculated to determine the proportion of shares of the second security versus one share of the first security
The resulting spread (combination of the two securities weighted by the hedge ratio) becomes a stationary series
The spread deviates significantly from its mean (exact deviation threshold not specified in excerpt)
exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: Apply PCA to identify factor returns with momentum, then construct a long-short portfolio by buying stocks with highest expected factor-based returns and shorting those with lowest expected returns.
entry_conditions: Apply PCA to S&P 600 small-cap stocks to extract principal components
Select top few eigenvectors (ranked by eigenvalues) to form factor matrix X
Assume factor returns exhibit momentum and remain constant from current to next period
Calculate expected returns for each stock based on factor exposures
Long: stocks with highest expected returns
Short: stocks with lowest expected returns
exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: Long the top N stocks with the highest expected 1-day returns based on factor exposures estimated over a lookback period.
entry_conditions: Calculate daily returns for all stocks in the lookback period (252 trading days)
Estimate factor exposures using the 5 factors and historical returns over the lookback period
Rank all stocks by expected 1-day forward returns based on factor model
Enter long positions in the top 50 stocks with highest expected 1-day returns
exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: A factor model strategy that identifies undervalued stocks by computing expected returns using principal component analysis on mean-centered historical returns, then ranks stocks by expected return to construct a portfolio.
entry_conditions: Calculate mean-centered returns over a lookback period for all stocks with complete data
Compute covariance matrix of returns and extract eigenvectors as factor exposures (X matrix)
Retain only the top eigenvectors corresponding to the numFactors largest eigenvalues
Perform ordinary least squares regression to estimate current factor returns (b) using most recent returns
Calculate expected returns for next period as: mean return + (factor exposures × estimated factor returns)
Rank stocks by expected returns in ascending order to identify candidates for long (highest expected) and short (lowest expected) positions
exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: Rank stocks by expected returns and simultaneously short the bottom N performers while buying the top N performers in a market-neutral pairs strategy.
entry_conditions: Sort all stocks by expected returns in ascending order
Short the topN stocks with the lowest expected returns
Buy the topN stocks with the highest expected returns
Only enter positions on days where data is available for the stock

parameters.threshold: topN (exact value not specified in excerpt)

exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

source_citation: Quantitative Trading, Chan, Ch. (pages 8-204), discussing fixed holding period exit strategies for momentum models
raw_chunk_text:
Astopprice
A fixed holding period is the default exit strategy for any trad-
ing strategy, whether it is a momentum model, a reversal model, or
somekindofseasonaltradingstrategy,whichcanbeeithermomen-
rule_text: Exit momentum trades after a fixed holding period determined by the average lifetime of the information diffusion process, with the caveat that optimal holding periods decrease over time as information diffuses faster and more traders exploit the opportunity.
entry_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT
exit_conditions: Exit position after a fixed holding period
Optimal holding period equals the average lifetime of the information diffusion process (to be determined via backtest)
Reduce holding period over time as market conditions change and information diffuses faster

rule_text: Trade mean-reverting spreads in stock pairs by identifying when the spread deviates from its long-term mean and expecting exponential decay back to equilibrium within a calculable half-life period.
entry_conditions: Spread z(t) deviates from mean µ (long stock 1 minus short stock 2)
Spread exhibits mean-reverting behavior following Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process
Half-life of mean reversion is calculable and economically tradeable

parameters.half_life: ln(2)/θ, where θ is estimated via linear regression of daily spread changes against spread value

exit_conditions: Spread converges back to mean µ (expected within half-life period)
AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: Trade mean-reverting spreads by using the half-life of mean reversion to determine optimal holding periods for positions.
entry_conditions: Spread deviates significantly from its mean (triggering condition AUTHOR_SILENT)
Mean reversion is confirmed via time-series analysis using exponential decay parameter θ

parameters.half_life: calculated as ln(2)/θ, representing the expected time for spread to revert to half its initial deviation

exit_conditions: Hold position for duration approximately equal to the calculated half-life
Exit when spread reverts toward mean (AUTHOR_SILENT on exact reversion target)

rule_text: Identify mean-reverting spread pairs (GLD-GDX) and hold the position for approximately the calculated half-life duration before expecting profitability.
entry_conditions: Security or spread exhibits mean-reverting behavior (confirmed via Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model fit)

Calculate half-life of mean reversion using the formula: half-life = -ln(2) / theta, where theta is the mean reversion coefficient from OLS regression of price changes against lagged deviations from mean
exit_conditions: Exit after approximately the half-life period has elapsed (around 10 days for the GLD-GDX example)
AUTHOR_SILENT on stop-loss or profit-target rules

rule_text: Exit a mean-reverting position when price reaches the historical mean (µ) or when the half-life threshold is reached, whichever occurs first.
entry_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT - entry conditions not specified in excerpt

parameters.half_life: Used as exit signal threshold; exact value AUTHOR_SILENT

exit_conditions: Price reverts to the historical mean (µ) of the security
Time-based exit when half-life period expires
Exit triggered by whichever criterion is met first

source_citation: Quantitative Trading, Chan, Ch. (Quantitative Trading overview), p. 8-204

Suppose you are running a trading model, and you entered into
a position based on its signal. Some time later, you run this model
again.Ifyoufindthatthesignofthislatestsignalisoppositetoyour
original
rule_text: Exit an existing position when the trading model generates a signal in the opposite direction, either by closing the position flat or by reversing into the opposite position.
entry_conditions: Trading model generates an entry signal
exit_conditions: Latest model signal is opposite in direction to the existing position (e.g., buy signal when holding a short position)
Exit can be executed by closing to flat, or closing existing position and entering opposite position

raw_chunk_text:
an entry model also tells us whether a stop-loss strategy is recom-
mended. In a momentum model, when a more recent entry signal
is opposite to an existing position, it means that the direction of
mom
rule_text: Exit a momentum position when a new entry signal in the opposite direction occurs, treating the reversal signal as a dynamic stop-loss mechanism.
entry_conditions: Initial momentum signal established in one direction (long or short)
exit_conditions: Most recent entry signal reverses to opposite direction
Exit is triggered automatically by momentum reversal without imposing arbitrary stop-loss price

source_citation: Quantitative Trading, Chan, Ch. (Seasonal Trading Strategies), p. 8-204
raw_chunk_text:
Consider a parallel situation when we are running a reversal
model. If an existing position has incurred a loss, running the re-
versalmodel again will simply generatea newsignalwith the same
sign.Thu
rule_text: In mean-reversion models, exit positions based on holding period or profit target rather than stop-loss, as mean-reversion signals should not be exited on losses.
entry_conditions: Mean-reversion entry signal is generated (price reverts toward mean)
exit_conditions: Position held for a predetermined holding period has elapsed
Profit target or profit cap is reached
Exit only on stop-loss if sudden regime shift to momentum is confirmed by recent news

SEASONAL TRADING STRATEGIES
This type of trading strategy is also called the calendar effect. Gen-
erally,thesestrategiesrecommendthatyoubuyorsellcertainsecu-
ritiesatafixeddateofeveryyear,andclosethe
rule_text: Buy small-cap stocks that had the worst returns in the prior year at a fixed calendar date, and close the position at another fixed calendar date.
entry_conditions: Security is a small-cap stock
Security had the worst returns in the prior calendar year
Entry occurs on a fixed date each year (specific date not stated in excerpt)
exit_conditions: Position is closed on a fixed date each year (specific date not stated in excerpt)

raw_chunk_text:
sion states that small-cap stocks that had the worst returns in the
previous calendar year will have higher returns in January than
small-cap stocks that had the best returns (Singal, 2006). The ra-
t
rule_text: Small-cap stocks with the worst prior-year returns outperform those with the best prior-year returns in January due to tax-loss-selling reversal.
entry_conditions: Select small-cap stocks ranked in the lowest decile of returns over the previous calendar year
Enter positions at or near the start of January
exit_conditions: Exit at or near end of January (implied seasonal window)
AUTHOR_SILENT for stop-loss or profit-taking levels

rule_text: Trade the January effect by taking positions in S&P 600 small-cap stocks based on seasonal patterns observed in January, with mean-reversion properties that benefit from market downturns and subsequent recoveries.
entry_conditions: Calendar month is January
Security is in S&P 600 small-cap index

parameters.backtest_period: Includes period around August 2007 (Federal Reserve emergency 75-basis-point rate cut)

exit_conditions: Calendar month transitions out of January

source_citation: Quantitative Trading, Chan, Ch. Special Topics in Quantitative Trading, p. 145
rule_text: Calculate annual returns by comparing closing prices at year-end dates, establishing a framework for measuring year-over-year performance.
entry_conditions: Identify the last day of December (when month equals 12 and next day month equals 1)
Identify the last day of January (when month equals 1 and next day month equals 2)
Ensure each last day of January occurs after the corresponding last day of December
Identify end-of-year indices where the year changes to the next year
Verify end-of-year dates align with last days of December
exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: A contrarian strategy that longs stocks with the lowest prior-year returns and shorts stocks with the highest prior-year returns, holding for one month (January) and rebalancing annually.
entry_conditions: Identify stocks in the lowest decile (bottom 10%) based on prior calendar year's total returns
Identify stocks in the highest decile (top 10%) based on prior calendar year's total returns
Enter long positions in lowest-decile stocks on the first trading day of January
Enter short positions in highest-decile stocks on the first trading day of January

parameters.threshold: 10th percentile (decile) for longs; 90th percentile (decile) for shorts

exit_conditions: Exit all positions on the last trading day of January
Rebalance portfolio annually at the start of each January based on prior-year returns

recently (Heston and Sadka, 2007; available at lcb1.uoregon.

rule_text: Each month, buy the stocks that had the best performance in the same calendar month one year earlier, and simultaneously short the stocks that had the worst performance in that same month one year earlier.
entry_conditions: Identify the N stocks with the highest returns in month M of the prior year
Identify the N stocks with the lowest returns in month M of the prior year
At the start of month M in the current year, enter long positions in the top performers
At the start of month M in the current year, enter short positions in the worst performers

parameters.backtest_period: pre-2002 (average annual return >13% before transaction costs); effect has disappeared since 2002

exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

SeasonalTrendingStrategy
Here is the MATLAB code for the year-on-year seasonal trending strategy
I quoted earlier. (The source code can be downloaded from epchan.com/
book/example7 7.m. The data is al
rule_text: A seasonal trending strategy that ranks stocks by their month-over-month returns and trades based on year-on-year seasonal patterns in the S&P 500 constituents.
entry_conditions: Identify the last trading day of each month
Calculate monthly returns for each stock as (current month-end close - prior month-end close) / prior month-end close
Sort stocks by monthly returns in ascending order to identify relative performance rankings
Enter positions based on seasonal patterns identified from historical year-on-year return rankings

parameters.backtest_period: Based on S&P 500 constituents as of November 23, 2007

exit_conditions: AUTHOR_SILENT

rule_text: Monthly momentum strategy that goes long the top decile of stocks by previous year returns and short the bottom decile, rebalancing monthly.
entry_conditions: Rank all stocks by their monthly returns over the previous 12 months
Identify the top 10% of stocks by past 12-month performance and take long positions
Identify the bottom 10% of stocks by past 12-month performance and take short positions
Rebalance positions at the start of each month based on prior year ranking
Only include stocks with valid return data in the prior year and valid closing price at month end
exit_conditions: Monthly rebalancing: close all positions and rerank stocks based on new 12-month return period

raw_chunk_text:
One of the most intuitive commodity seasonal trades is the
gasoline future trade: Simply buy the gasoline future contract that
expires in May near the middle of April, and sell it by the end
of April.
rule_text: Buy May gasoline futures contract in mid-April and sell by end of April to capture seasonal summer driving season price appreciation
entry_conditions: Calendar month is April
Time period is middle of April (approximately mid-month)
Instrument is gasoline futures contract expiring in May
exit_conditions: Exit by end of April (approximately 2 weeks after entry)
target_regimes: Seasonal uptrend in spring driving season

rule_text: Construct a leveraged low-beta portfolio to achieve higher Sharpe ratio by reallocating from traditional high-risk asset allocation to a risk-parity allocation with leverage.
entry_conditions: Identify assets with low beta (systematic risk below market average)
Allocate 23% to stocks and 77% to bonds instead of traditional 60-40 split
Apply 1.8x leverage to the entire rebalanced portfolio to match risk level of original 60-40 allocation

rule_text: Prefer low-beta stocks over high-beta stocks and leverage the low-beta portfolio to achieve maximum compounded growth rate, exploiting chronic market underpricing of high-beta equities.
entry_conditions: Identify stocks with low beta relative to market
Construct portfolio of low-beta stocks
Apply leverage to low-beta portfolio
